skill.name: microsoft-azure-webjobs-extensions-authentication-events-dotnet
skill.description: Guidance for building Microsoft Entra Authentication Events handlers with Azure Functions and the .NET WebJobs extension.
skill.summary: Build .NET Azure Functions for Microsoft Entra custom authentication events.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The static command-execution findings are false positives from Markdown fences and inline package-install examples in SKILL.md. The weak-crypto and reconnaissance alerts are also false positives, but the guide includes examples that call an external user-profile API and log a phone number in an authentication flow. Publish with a warning that copied implementations must protect authentication data, avoid unnecessary external disclosure, and limit PII logging.

medium_findings.title: External User Profile Lookup in Authentication Flow
medium_findings.description: The sample token-enrichment handler sends the authentication user ID to an external API endpoint. This is a legitimate integration pattern, but copied code could disclose identifiers to third-party systems or add fragile dependencies to sign-in unless implementers add consent, access controls, timeout handling, and data minimization.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code clearly builds an HTTP request to an external user-profile service with the user ID in the path. The endpoint is a placeholder, so this is a copy-risk and privacy risk rather than evidence of malicious exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: PII Logging Example in OTP Handler
medium_findings.description: The OTP sample logs the destination phone number after sending a one-time password. Phone numbers are sensitive authentication data, and production implementations should log masked identifiers or correlation IDs instead.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sample stores the OTP destination in phoneNumber and logs that value on success. This is direct evidence of a privacy issue if the sample is copied into production.

low_findings.title: False Positive Markdown Command Execution Detections
low_findings.description: The many Ruby or shell backtick detections correspond to Markdown code fences, inline code spans, C# examples, JSON examples, and package installation snippets. No executable script file, dynamic command construction, or hidden command runner was found in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged backticks are visible Markdown formatting and documentation snippets. I found no code path that executes shell commands from the skill itself.

low_findings.title: False Positive Weak Cryptography Detections
low_findings.description: The high-severity weak-cryptography alerts map to the description and a JSON deserialization line, not to MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or similar cryptographic usage. No evidence found of weak cryptographic algorithm recommendations in the reviewed file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain front matter text and JsonSerializer.Deserialize. The static labels do not match the actual content at those locations.

low_findings.title: Documentation Links and Placeholder URLs
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings are mostly Microsoft, NuGet, and GitHub documentation links. The example.com API URL is a placeholder in sample code, but implementers should replace it with approved internal services.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The links are plainly references or a placeholder API in an instructional Markdown file. There is no evidence of a live malicious endpoint or automatic network access by the skill.

low_findings.title: False Positive System Reconnaissance Detection
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance alert maps to a best-practice sentence about caching external data. No host inventory, environment probing, or system discovery instruction was found at the cited location.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited line is a performance recommendation, not a command or reconnaissance workflow. The surrounding best-practices list does not request system enumeration.

dangerous_patterns.title: HTTP Request Built from Authentication User Identifier
dangerous_patterns.description: The guide shows an HTTP GET to an external profile service with the current user ID in the URL path. This pattern can be acceptable with strict governance, but it should be reviewed for privacy, latency, and failure behavior before production use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The HTTP call and user ID input are explicit in the code sample. The risk depends on deployment choices, so confidence is high for the pattern and moderate for practical impact.

content.value_statement: Custom authentication events need careful .NET Azure Functions patterns. This skill provides focused examples for token claims, attribute collection, OTP delivery, and Entra configuration.
actual_capabilities: Shows how to install the Microsoft.Azure.WebJobs.Extensions.AuthenticationEvents package.
Provides .NET examples for token issuance custom claims.
Demonstrates attribute collection start and submit event handlers.
Outlines custom OTP delivery response patterns.
Lists Entra ID configuration steps and related SDK references.
Includes basic error handling and monitoring guidance.
limitations: It is documentation only and does not include runnable project files.
External API and SMS provider integrations are placeholders.
It does not verify current Azure portal screens or service limits.
It does not include automated tests or deployment scripts.

use_cases.title: Add Claims During Sign-In
use_cases.description: Use the token issuance examples to plan custom claims for Entra access or ID tokens.

use_cases.title: Customize Sign-Up Attributes
use_cases.description: Use the attribute collection examples to prefill, validate, or modify user-submitted profile data.

use_cases.title: Review Authentication Extension Designs
use_cases.description: Use the configuration notes and patterns to review data flow, logging, and extension behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Token Handler
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to draft a .NET Azure Function for OnTokenIssuanceStart that adds department and cost center claims.

prompt_templates.title: Validate Attribute Collection Input
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design an OnAttributeCollectionSubmit handler that blocks invalid email domains and returns user-friendly validation errors.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Custom OTP Delivery
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to outline a custom OnOtpSend implementation with provider error handling, masked logging, and retry boundaries.

prompt_templates.title: Assess Production Readiness
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to review my Entra authentication extension design for privacy, timeout, logging, and failure-mode risks.

output_examples.input: I need to add employee metadata to Entra tokens.
output_examples.output: A concise implementation plan with the required package, trigger type, response action, claim names, and security checks for external profile data.

output_examples.input: I need to validate sign-up attributes before saving them.
output: Notes about logging and correlation IDs

output_examples.input: I need a readiness review for an OTP customization.
output_examples.output: A risk-focused checklist covering provider reliability, secret handling, masked logs, timeout behavior, and failure responses.

best_practices: Validate every request field before using it in claims, profile updates, or provider calls.
Use managed identity and approved internal services for external data lookups.
Log correlation IDs and masked identifiers instead of raw authentication data.
anti_patterns: Do not put unverified external data directly into tokens.
Do not log OTP codes, full phone numbers, or unnecessary user identifiers.
Do not make authentication depend on slow services without timeout and fallback rules.

faq.question: Does this skill run Azure commands?
faq.answer: No. It is a Markdown guide with package, C#, JSON, and portal configuration examples.

faq.question: Which Entra events are covered?
faq.answer: It covers token issuance, attribute collection start, attribute collection submit, and OTP send events.

faq.question: Can I use the external API example directly?
faq.answer: Treat it as a placeholder. Use approved services, protect identifiers, and add timeout and failure handling.

faq.question: Does it include a complete Azure Functions project?
faq.answer: No. It provides focused examples and configuration notes, not a full project structure.

faq.question: Is it suitable for production security review?
faq.answer: It can support review, but it does not replace threat modeling, privacy review, or Azure configuration validation.

faq.question: What tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
